coredata.dc:title: Photo-electrodes decorated with carbon quantum dots: Efficient dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheruku, Rajesh
Kim, Jae Hong
Krishna, V.B Murali
Periyat, P.
SSSR, Sarathbabu Duvvuri
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the blue and green carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized and applied to the photo-electrodes of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The DSSCs exhibited high power conversion efficiency because of the conversion of ultraviolet to the visible region in photo-anode, and good catalytic activity in the photo-cathode. The blue color CQDs (BCQDs) and green color CQDs (GCQDs) are synthesized from the citric acid in a single pot process. We examine the influence of both CQDs decorated on photo-anode and photo-cathode and both photo-electrodes on the photovoltaic performance of the DSSCs. Furthermore, the DSSCs are studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, and Tafel polarization data to study the charge transfer kinetics. The better performance observed from the DSSCs due to enhanced charge carrier collection through the CQDs. The photovoltaic results of DSSCs shown that the best efficiency shown by DSSC, which fabricated by both photo-electrodes decorated by CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Light absorption
Carbon quantum dots
Photosensitizer
Dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) X-ray diffraction data, (b) Raman spectra, (c) Fourier transform infrared spectra, and (d) Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra and photoluminescence data of blue carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) and green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs).
(a) and (d) Full range X-ray photoelectron spectra of BCQDs and GCQDs. C 1s electronic states of (b) BCQDs and (e) GCQDs. O1s electronic peaks of (c) BCQDs and (f) GCQDs.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of (a) BCQDs and (c) GCQDs. Insets show the size distribution of the CQDs. (c) and (d) 20 nm TEM images. Insets show the corresponding d-spacing.

(a) and (c) J–V curves of all the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) (b) and (d) Comparison of incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency spectra of the DSSCs with and without CQDs on photo-electrodes.

(a) and (c) Nyquist plots of all DSSCs. Inset shows the equivalent circuit model used to obtain all the DSSCs data. (b) and (d) show the Bode electrochemical impedance spectroscopy phase plots of DSSCs with and without CQDs on photo-electrodes in a frequency ranging from 0.1 to 500 kHz at room temperature.

Cyclic voltammetry data of (a) photo-anodes and (b) photo-cathodes for the
I 3 −
/
I −
redox species at a scanning rate of 100 mV s−1 (c) Tafel data of Photo-cathodes of DSSCs.
Performance parameters of DSSCs with and without CQDs in different photo-electrodes.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy parameter values of DSSCs with and without CQDs in different photo-electrodes.

head.ce:title: Photo-electrodes decorated with carbon quantum dots: Efficient dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Physics, School of Engineering, Dayananda Sagar University, Bangalore, 560 068, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the blue and green carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are synthesized and applied to the photo-electrodes of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The DSSCs exhibited high power conversion efficiency because of the conversion of ultraviolet to the visible region in photo-anode, and good catalytic activity in the photo-cathode. The blue color CQDs (BCQDs) and green color CQDs (GCQDs) are synthesized from the citric acid in a single pot process. We examine the influence of both CQDs decorated on photo-anode and photo-cathode and both photo-electrodes on the photovoltaic performance of the DSSCs. Furthermore, the DSSCs are studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, and Tafel polarization data to study the charge transfer kinetics. The better performance observed from the DSSCs due to enhanced charge carrier collection through the CQDs. The photovoltaic results of DSSCs shown that the best efficiency shown by DSSC, which fabricated by both photo-electrodes decorated by CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy needs for humans are intensified by economic expansion and social progress. Therefore, the traditional nonrenewable fossil fuels are intensely debilitated, hence provoking the energy problem as a significant perturb that should be resolved urgently. For solving this issue, scientists have inspected the availability of different types of energy sources, such as water, geothermal, solar, and wind. Among them, solar energy is identified as the key solution for achieving a renewable energy source because of geographical distribution, sustainability, and cost. Researchers have investigated different solar energy conversion methods over the years. Among them, silicon solar cells (SSCs) have shown a high efficiency. However, these cells demonstrate an inadequate performance under diffused sunlight and incur high manufacturing costs, thereby obstruct their extensive application [1–3]. The third-generation solar cells, called dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), have been developed to overcome these problems. DSSCs exhibit the ability to exceed SSCs due to their ease of fabrication, low processing costs, better performance during cloudy days, and moderate efficiency [2]. DSSCs generally comprise a photo-anode (TiO2), photosensitization agents (dye), Redox couple (electrolyte), and a counter electrode (CE) (Pt) [4,5]. In SSCs, silicon provides the source for photo-electrons and functions as a charge transport carrier. Therefore, such cells do not function properly under diffused sunlight. However, in DSSCs, the semiconductor (TiO2) acts as a charge carrier, while the dye acts as a photon collector. Under diffused sunlight, the photosensitizing dye molecules are sufficiently sensitive to gather the photons, therefore providing major advantages.

Studies have introduced numerous approaches to improve the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of DSSC by changing its components. In this regard, photo-anode alteration is one of the best methods to design different redox couples by lowering the potential. The molecular design of the efficient dye and modification of the CE configuration enables cheaper and more efficient materials as replacements for the expensive Pt [3]. In addition, the photo-anode in the DSSC functions as a light-harvesting part and a charge carrier. Hypothetically, it plays a critical role in improving the PCE. TiO2 is the normally used photo-anode because of its relatively high specific surface area, high energy conversion efficiency, low cost, and non-toxicity. However, it demonstrates a major limitation in terms of random electron transport that leads to poor performance of the device due to the recombination of the electron-hole pair [4]. Therefore, since several years, researchers have focused on continuous efforts to enhance the performance of DSSCs [2]. Gao et al. have attained an efficiency of 11.1 % after modifying the dye, and Yella et al. [5] have modified cobalt (II/III) electrolyte to obtain an efficiency of 12.3 % [6]. However, the cobalt-based electrolyte is not ecologically friendly and incurs marginally higher costs. Kenji et al. have reported the highest efficiency of 14.3 % with a collaborative sensitization of ADEKA-1 and LEG4 dyes [7]. Research works have been attempting to enhance the stability and permanence of the electrolyte using ionic liquids and gel-based electrolytes, which are effective during sunny days [8]. Among the components modification strategies implemented on the DSSCs, the modification of photo-anode has not been as successful as those of the dye and electrolyte [9,10]. Therefore, the efficiency of DSSCs has been improved by enhancing the photo-anode configuration, which has rendered them reliable for a wider utilization. In addition, a cost-effective DSSC has been fabricated by replacing the expensive Pt CE with a low cost, good conductive, and highly stable PEDOT CE [11,12]. Moreover, size-selective quantum dots (QDs) have been loaded over photo-electrodes to further improve the PCE of DSSC, and this enhancement has been performed by with size quantization effects, which aids in tuning the bandgap.

The main advantage of QDs in the photo-anode is to enhance the photon collection in the ultraviolet region [13]. In this regard, carbon QDs (CQDs)-modified photo-anodes for DSSC applications show a low recombination rate, improved dye adsorption, and a quick electron transport in the device. Therefore, the PCE of DSSC has been enhanced by a synergistic effect among the carbon/semiconductor network [14,15]. The CQDs might be the favored materials owing to not only their low toxicity, chemical inertness, good photo-stability, but also their easy functionalization. In several aspects, CQDs can replace the renowned QDs based on metal chalcogenides because of their outstanding biocompatibility and low-cost synthesis methods. Therefore, CQDs are utilized in different fields of science, e.g., photo-electrochemical water splitting [16,17], in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging [18,19], light-emitting diodes [19,20], photo-catalysis [21] and bio-sensing [22]. The CQDs demonstrate a spherical shape with a diameter of up to 10 nm. The bottom-up synthetic strategies were mostly used for their preparation [19].

The fluorescence emissions and optical absorption in CQDs do not emerge from a bandgap, but result due to the π -plasmon and the radiative recombination of surface-confined holes and electrons. Currently, several efforts have been focused on exposing the origin of photoluminescence (PL) of the CQDs. This has been investigated through the examination of numerous aspects, such as the type of carbon backbone [23], size of the graphitic core [24], and the type and quantity of the functional groups [25]. These excellent properties of CQDs enable them to become good light absorbers in broadening the spectral absorption and collecting the charges [26]. In the present study, the environment-friendly CQDs (blue and green) were prepared from citric acid and loaded on TiO2 (anode/electrolyte interface), PEDOT (counter/electrolyte interface), and both electrodes of the DSSC. To the best of our knowledge, our design, which comprises the application of both CQDs over the photo-electrodes of the DSSC, is the first of its kind. These CQDs provide a superfast connection to facilitate hole injection in the electrolyte and light converter and accelerate the electron transfer rate in the counter/electrolyte interface.

Citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, >99 %), and lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, 97 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The 3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene (EDOT, 97 %) and TBAPF6 (Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. The FTO glass substrates with a sheet resistance of 8 Ω/cm2 and thickness of 2.3 mm were obtained from TEC8, Pilkington. The Surlyn® (60 μm) and ruthenium dyes (N719) were purchased from Solaronix. Hexahydrate (H2PtCl6), and the chloroplatinic acid was obtained from Dyesol. Transparent TiO2 (20 nm) paste and scattering TiO2 (250 nm) paste were purchased from E&B Korea. All chemicals were reagent grade and utilized as obtained without any extra purification.

The blue CQDs (BCQDs) and green CQDs (GCQDs) were prepared by mixing 1.5 g and 0.7 g of citric acid in 5 ml of water, respectively. For BCQDs, the pH of the solution was maintained acidic by the adding HCl drop by drop. Then the solution was heated for 60 min at 90 °C [27]. After allowing the yellow/brown solution to cool down to the room temperature, it was neutralized by adding the NaOH solution drop by drop. In contrast, for the GCQDs, the pH of the solution was maintained basic by adding the NaOH solution. The resultant solution was heated for 20 min at 90 °C. The CQD solutions obtained were filtered, centrifuged (10,000 rpm), and dialyzed for three days in a dialysis bag (3500 D). Then the final aqueous CQD solutions were obtained, which were freeze-dried in vacuum to obtain solid powders [28]. The synthesis procedure of both CQDs and corresponding images of the diluted nanoparticles in deionized (DI) water under ambient and UV light are shown in Fig. S1.

The FTO transparent glass was cleaned chronologically using DI water, detergent, ethanol, and acetone with ultrasonic wringing for 30 min and then dehydrated in the oven. For improving the adhesion between FTO and active TiO2 and repress the electron back transport by the TiO2 blocking layer. Therefore, a compact layer was coated on the FTO glass substrates by spin-coating titanium (IV) isopropoxide (2 wt%) solution for 30 s at a speed of 1500 RPM, which was then sintered at 450 °C for 30 min. Next, the TiO2 (20 nm) active layer was coated using the doctor-blade technique, and the substrates were calcined for 30 min in a muffle furnace at 450 °C. The cross-section, morphology, and size of the TiO2 nanoparticles of the films are illustrated in Figs. S2a–c. These images reveal that the size of the blocking layer and active layer are approximately 116 nm and 6.08 μm, respectively, and the average size of a TiO2 nanoparticle is 30 nm after sintering. Subsequently, a TiO2 (250 nm) scattering layer was deposited onto the substrates by a doctor-blade technique and further sintered in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 30 min. The morphology of the film is depicted in Fig. S2d. 0.5 mm of N719 ruthenium dye in ethanol solution was loaded on the as-prepared TiO2 film by immersing the film completely into the solution at ambient temperature for 24 h. After loading the dye molecules, the TiO2 film was cleaned with ethanol to eliminate the non-adsorbed dye molecules from the surface. The N719 ruthenium dye-sensitized TiO2 film thus obtained was used as the photo-anode for DSSC.

The PEDOT CE was prepared by the one-step electrochemical polymerization method [29]. The deposition solution comprised 0.01 M EDOT monomer, 0.1 M LiClO4, and 0.05 M SDS in DI water. In this method, a three-electrode arrangement was utilized by the FTO glass (working electrode), Ag/Ag+ (reference electrode), and Pt mesh (CE). PEDOT was deposited over the FTO glass by applying a potential of 1 V for 30 s. The schematic working principle and experimental setup are shown in Figs. S3a and S3b. Then the polymerized PEDOT CE was cleaned with DI water and ethanol. The subsequent CEs were utilized in the DSSCs. The field emission scanning electron microscope cross-section view of the PEDOT CE is depicted in Fig. S3c. The PEDOT CE and the molecular structure of PEDOT are shown in Figs. S3d and S3e, respectively. All the photo-electrodes were spin-coated by CQD (BCQDs/GCQDs) solutions (1 mg/ml in ethanol) for 30 s at 2000 rpm to obtain CQD-sensitized photo-electrodes. The DSSC was assembled by sandwiching Surlyn® (60 μm) between a photo-anode and a photo-cathode loaded with and without the CQDs. The cell was then heated for 1 min at 120 °C to seal both the photo-electrodes. Afterward, a liquid electrolyte (0.2 M LiI, 0.5 M t-butylpyridine, 0.05 M I2, and 0.7 M 3-propyl-1-methyl imidazolium iodide dissolved in acetonitrile) was added into the photo-electrodes through pre-drilled holes in the cell. The active area of each cell was 0.24 cm2 (0.4 cm × 0.6 cm). Fig. S4 illustrates the step by step experimental method used for preparing the CQD-loaded DSSC.

The crystalline nature of CQDs was examined through a PANalytical X-ray diffractometer, and their molecular bonding properties were inspected through attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of CQDs were verified using Agilent 8453 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were collected on the JASCO FP-6500 Spectrofluorometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of CQDs were recorded using a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The baseline of the spectra was revised by the instrument's software. The morphology and cross-section images of the photo-anode were obtained through field emission scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi FE-SEM S4800). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using the Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN instrument (200 kV) on a Cu grid. Image J software was used to deduce the d-spacing and particle size distribution of the CQDs. The J–V characteristics of the fabricated DSSCs were analyzed under a radiation of 1 sun (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data was obtained by an electrochemical analyzer (Iviumstat Tec.). The Zview2 commercial software (Scribner Associates Inc.) was used to analyze the data. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) data of the DSSC were collected from the electrochemical analyzer (Iviumstat Tec.). The Tafel data of the CQDs were verified on the similar workstation at a voltage of -1–1 V and a scan rate of 10 mV s−1.

The crystalline nature of the synthesized CQDs was examined using the XRD data. Fig. 1 a depicts the diffraction pattern of BCQDs and GCQDs. A single broad high-intensity peak is observed in the range of 15°–25°, which signifies the (002) plane of bulk graphite with the interlayer spacing of 4.2 Å (BCQDs) and 4.3 Å (GCQDs) [30]. The broad nature of the peak represents the fractional graphitization and small size of the synthesized BCQDs and GCQDs [31]. In addition, the nanocrystalline nature of the prepared BCQDs and GCQDs was observed from the low intensity and high full-width half maxima of the maximum intensity peak in the XRD data [32]. The presence of the functional group over both CQDs was identified by the Raman and FTIR data.

The Raman spectra for BCQDs and GCQDs were recorded to depict the crystallinity of the synthesized carbon nanostructures, and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. This figure shows two distinct peaks at 1352 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 for BCQDs and 1375 cm−1 and 1561 cm−1 for GCQDs. The D band originates because of the A1g breathing mode. It shows the existence of defects, which originate from transverse optical phonons adjacent to the first Brillouin zone K points. The G band relates to sp2 carbon clusters, and it appears because of the Brillouin zone E2g phonon mode [33]. The defect density (ID/IG ratio) values for BCQDs and GCQDs were calculated to be 1.77 and 0.85, respectively. This indicates that the BCQDs demonstrate a highly disordered structure than the GCQDs, which can be attributed to the existence of extra oxygen holding functional groups in the synthesized BGQDs. An increase in the oxygen functional groups on the surface directs the surface oxidation, thus increasing the water solubility of CQDs [34].

The FTIR spectra were utilized to describe the existence of different functional groups in both CQDs, and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 1c. A board absorption band present in the range of 3100–3600 cm−1 and 2872 cm−1 in the spectra can be attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and C–H functional groups [35]. The presence of these functional groups is accountable for the good stability and hydrophilicity of the CQDs in water. The peak observed at 1731 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO carboxylic functional groups [36]. Another two major peaks at 1409 cm−1 and 1067 cm−1 indicate the existence of CC vibrational stretches ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic structures and C–O–C molecular vibrations, respectively, representing that the surfaces of the both CQDs are partially oxidized. Furthermore, the existence of C–O–C and CO functional groups can be a promising cause for the long-range emission of both CQDs [37]. The existence of these functional groups contributes to the amphiphilic properties of the CQDs [38]. The reliability of the FTIR studies were further confirmed through the XPS analysis of both CQDs.

To examine the optical characteristics of both CQDs, UV–Vis and PL studies were conducted in detail. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra were recorded to examine the electronic transitions in the prepared BCQDs and GCQDs. Fig. 1d shows the UV–Vis spectra of BCQDs and GCQDs dispersed in DI water. The spectra of the BCQDs and GCQDs exhibit distinct peaks at 255 and 257 nm, respectively, owing to the π−π* transitions. The relatively weak absorption at 361 and 357 nm in the spectra show the n–π* transitions of CO bonds in the prepared CQDs. The π−π* transitions can be attributed to the electronic transitions inside the carbon core that includes sp2 associated carbogenic domains. However, the n–π* band detected in the UV spectra can result from the trapping of excited-state energy through the surface states on the CQDs [39,40]. The absorption spectra extends to high wavelength regions due to several low-energy transitions that occur in functional groups present on the surface of the CQDs [41]. The well-dispersed solution of CQDs demonstrate blue and green emissions under UV light, which can be simply detected by the eye. The inset in Fig. 1d was captured by a digital camera.

PL can be considered as a more sensitive parameter to examine the luminescence behavior of the CQDs in solution [41]. It is clear from Fig. 1d that both CQDs show a strong fluorescence emission at 440 (BCQDs) and 510 (GCQDs) nm at the excitation of 370 and 400 nm, respectively. In addition, the emission spectra were tested at varied excitation range from 250 to 450 nm with an interval of 50 nm, and the corresponding data and normalized intensity data are shown in Figs. S5a–d. These data of both CQDs indicate that the excitation depends on emission wavelength. In the fluorescence spectra of both CQDs in water, a redshift can be observed, when the excitation wavelength is amplified from 250 to 450 nm. Furthermore, the intensity of the emission peak increases on the excitation of both CQDs at higher wavelengths due to their similar size and the minimal scattering of emissive trap states on their surfaces [42]. These data confirm that both CQDs demonstrate good optical properties, which influences the PCE of the DSSCs.

The XPS analysis was performed to investigate the surface states and elemental conformation of the prepared BCQDs and GCQDs. Fig. 2 a and d shows the overall XPS spectra of the BCQDs and GCQDs. The de-convoluted C1s and O1s XPS spectra of the BCQDs are shown in Fig. 2b and c, and those of the GCQDs are shown in Fig. 2e and f. The XPS spectra confirm that the as-prepared BCQDs and GCQDs mainly comprise carbon and oxygen. The de-convoluted C1s spectra of BCQDs exhibit peaks at 284.1, 285.7, and 287.2 eV, while GCQDs exhibit peaks at 284.1, 285.5, and 286.2 eV corresponding to the presence of sp2 (C–C), sp3 (CC/C–O), and CO carbons [43,44]. These data confirm that the oxygen-containing functional groups are present in both CQDs. Further, the atomic percentages of carbon and oxygen elements are observed to be 65.15 % and 34.85 % for BCQDs and 66.95 % and 33.05 % for GCQDs. Fig. 2c and f depict the O1s spectra, as observed in the functional groups CO and C–OH/C–O–C on the surface of the BCQDs and GCQDs [45,46]. These results reveal that the synthesized BCQDs and GCQDs are rich in oxygen functional groups.

The HRTEM images shown in Fig. 3 confirm that both CQDs possess an average particle sizes of under 5 nm. Fig. 3a and c shows the HRTEM images of both CQDs with a 5 nm scale, and the corresponding insets show the particle size histogram of both CQDs, derived from the pictorial data obtained from the TEM images. These images reveal that the symmetrical size distribution are 3.87 ± 0.03 and 4.21 ± 0.02 nm for BCQDs and GCQDs, respectively. The HRTEM images of both CQDs with a 20 nm scale are shown in Fig. 3b and d, which reveal the uniform distribution of CQDs without any aggregation. The insets show that both CQDs demonstrate an equal d-spacing of 0.21 nm, thereby matching with the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [47]. Figs. S6 and S7 show the high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM. The resultant images of carbon and oxygen mapping clearly confirm the absence of impure elements in both CQDs. The HRTEM data confirm that both CQDs demonstrate a good size distribution of graphitic carbon in the nanometer range.

Fig. 4 shows the schematic illustration of the CQDs loaded over the photo-electrodes of the DSSC. The photocurrent density–photovoltage data of the DSSCs with both CQDs were measured by voltage scanning the cell under the illumination of standard AM 1.5 condition, and the current was measured with a potentiostat/sourcemeter. The PCE values of the DSSCs with both CQDs were calculated from the ratio of the device maximum power to the incoming light intensity. Fig. 5 a and c shows the J–V curves of photovoltaic cells with and without both CQDs spin-coated on electrodes, and the resultant photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table 1 . The devices with both sides coated CQD electrodes demonstrate the best PCE compared to the bare and single side coated electrodes. The PCE of the device with BCQDs coated on both electrodes is 7.21 % with an open-circuit voltage (V oc) of 0.7 V, short-circuit current (J sc) of 14.41 mA cm−2, and a fill factor (FF) of 71 %. Similarly, the PCE of the device with GCQDs coated on both electrodes is 7.3 % with an open-circuit voltage (V oc) of 0.7 V, short-circuit current (J sc) of 14.7 mA cm−2, and an FF of 70 %. This improvement may be due to the PL property of the CQDs, as this property can improve the photon emanation to a visible region, when the UV light is excited on solar cells [48,49]. Therefore, CQDs can generate more electron-hole pairs in the photo-electrodes and improve the J
sc and PCE of DSSCs. The J
sc enhancement in DSSCs with both sides coated electrodes significant to the enhancement of light absorption measured by UV–vis is shown in Figs. S8a and S8b. The V
oc of all DSSCs are normally equal to 0.70 V owing to the constant potential difference between the electrolyte and dye sensitizer. The FF values among DSSCs are almost equal, which confirms that there is no agglomeration of CQDs on the photo-electrode surface [31,50,51].

The IPCE of DSSCs defines their spectral response to entering unicolor light in terms of current. Fig. 5b and d shows the IPCE spectra of DSSCs with and without the CQDs on the photo-electrodes. The IPCE of the DSSCs with CQDs on both photo-electrodes at wavelengths between 300 and 800 nm is 65 %, which is higher than those of the bare and individual loaded photo-electrode DSSCs. This improvement evidences an effective photon-to-electron conversion. The IPCE value is improved not only in the UV region, but also in a visible range, which can be ascribed to strong visible photon absorption. The IPCE study also supports our UV–vis absorption results of both photo-electrodes with and without CQDs (Fig. S8). The IPCE of the DSSC increases in case of both CQDs due to the increased charge regeneration and decreased series resistance, which is also confirmed by the impedance spectroscopy (section 3.3.3), CV, and Tafel measurements (sections 3.3.4).

The effect of CQDs on photo-electrodes of DSSCs was further investigated through EIS. The EIS measurements were conducted under illumination of 100 mW cm−2 from the frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz, and the Nyquist and Bode EIS phase plots of the DSSCs are shown in Fig. 6 . In Fig. 6a and c, each of the plots demonstrate two dispersed semicircles and a spike, and these data fit with the matching equivalent circuit, which includes electrical elements, such as resistors (R1, R2, and R3), constant phase elements (CPE1 and CPE2), and Warburg (Wo) impedances (depicted in the inset) [52]. In the equivalent circuit, R1 (series resistance) and the high-frequency dispersed semicircle denote the parallel combination of circuit components CPE1 (chemical capacitance) and R2 (bulk resistance), respectively, which correspond to photoexcitation in the DSSC. At low frequency, the second dispersed semicircle represents the parallel combination of CPE2 (chemical capacitance) and R3 (charge carrier transfer resistance), which are attributed to the internal charge transfer route at the donor/acceptor boundaries. Wo denotes the diffusion of charge in the DSSC. Table 2 lists the circuit elements data obtained by fitting the impedance spectra of the DSSC with and without CQDs. The DSSCs with CQDs in both photo-electrodes show low R2 and R3 values compared to the remaining DSSCs. This confirms that the photo-electrodes comprising both CQDs contribute to charger carrier transfer and reduce the recombination at the interface.

The lifetime of photo-electrons in the DSSC can be calculated with the equation τ = 1/2πfpeak where fpeak is the peak frequency. The Bode phase data of the DSSCs with both CQDs are shown in Fig. 6b and d. A higher τ value shows that the electrons undergo restricted backward recombination reactions with
I 3 −
species, thereby enhancing the TiO2 conduction band electron density for a high Jsc output. The values of τ for DSSCs with both CQDs is 2.4 ms (BCQDs) and 2.52 ms (GCQDs), which are higher than those of the remaining DSSCs. These data demonstrate the suppression of recombination reactions in the DSSCs. Therefore, these results are in good agreement with the obtained photovoltaic data of the DSSCs.

The CV and Tafel polarization curves were plotted to investigate more about the interfacial charge transfer kinetics and photoexcitation behavior of CQDs over electrodes. Fig. 7 shows the CV and Tafel data of photo-electrodes with and without CQDs. The CV data of both photo-electrodes (Fig. 7a and b) were observed with 0.1 M of TBAPF6 redox couples in acetonitrile solution and recorded at a scanning rate of 100 mVs−1. CQDs reduce TiO2 bandgap to increase UV–visible light absorption. Once energized, CQDs capture low-energy visible light photons and form photo-excited electrons, boosting electron transport to TiO2 conducting band. Improve current density and PCE. CQDs also minimize TiO2 optical band gap by suppressing electron-hole recombination and donating energy used to boost photovoltaics. Modification extends electron lifetime, which may reduce electron-hole pair recombination. As part of the photoelectric conversion process, CEs of DSSC are very important because they catch electrons and lower the overpotential so that
I 3 −
can be reduced to
I −
. So, the abundance of the electrons has a big effect on this process of changing the charge. The hybrid CEs does better on both series and charge diffusion resistances. This means that there is less electrical resistance and faster electron movement at the electrolyte contact. It has been shown that hybrid CEs are better at speeding up the
I 3 −
/
I −
redox reaction. This makes the dye regenerate faster and more photo-electrons are injected, which means more light is captured. Because of this, they got a PCE that was much higher than the standard device and a higher Jsc. The hybrid CEs is also very stable electrochemically over a long period of time in the
I 3 −
/
I −
, solution. When the excited dyes are brought back to their normal state, the
I −
ions in the electrolyte change into
I 3 −
ions and move to the interface between the CE and the electrolyte. The catalytic CE takes electrons from the outside circuit and lowers
I 3 −
to
I −
. In this process, the sun spectrum excites the CQDs, which creates photon-generated electrons that can be excited to take electrons from the CEs. The CEs surface that is rich in electrons helps the catalyst work better.

Fig. 7a shows the CV curves of the loaded and unloaded CQDs over photo-anodes with the oxidation (Ox) peaks from 0.7 to 1.3 V. The CQD-loaded photo-anodes show a higher current density than the bare one, and these results establish that the mixture of fluorescent CQDs with photo-electrodes can improve the catalytic activity. Similar behavior was observed in the CV curves of photo-cathodes with CQDs in the voltage range of −0.3–1 V (Fig. 7b). The photo-cathodes show two oxidation (Ox1, Ox2) and reduction (Red1, Red2) peaks with increasing current density and peak-to-peak separation due to the improved catalytic performance by the CQDs [53]. These results suggest that all the photo-electrodes improve their catalytic activity with the CQDs.

Fig. 7c shows the Tafel polarization data obtained with the symmetric dummy cell comprising two identical photo-cathodes to estimate the improvement of catalytic activities and interfacial charge transfer on the electrodes. Under the equilibrium state, the electron transfer rate may be evaluated from the joint of the linear cathodic and anodic branches of curves to show the exchange current density (J 0) [54]. The J 0 value of the cells with CQDs are approximately 7.8 mA cm−2 (GCQDs), 7.2 mA cm−2 (BCQDs), and 6.8 mA cm−2 (without CQDs), respectively. This indicates that the CQDs in the DSSC can handle the back reaction more efficiently and contribute toward improving the current in the device. In addition, more active sites and the high conductivity of the CQD-loaded photo-cathodes can be a vital influence in improving the catalytic activity of the DSSC [55]. Thus, these results show that the CV and Tafel data are in good agreement, thereby demonstrating the improved catalytic activity of the CQD-loaded photo-electrodes.

In this study, the blue and green CQDs were successfully synthesized with citric acid, and the synthesis was confirmed through XRD, Raman, FTIR, PL, and XPS. The nanocrystalline nature and size distribution of the CQDs were confirmed with the HR-TEM data. These synthesized CQDs were spin-coated efficiently over the photo-electrodes of DSSC to improve the PCE. The devices with CQDs loaded on both photo-electrodes demonstrate better photo-response properties, photo-conversion efficiency, and IPCE, compared to the ones with bare and single photo-electrode loaded CQDs. This enhancement in the efficiency of DSSCs was attributed to the higher number of photo-generated electrons from CQDs. The EIS, CV, and Tafel measurements confirmed that the CQD-loaded photo-electrodes demonstrate less charge transfer resistance and better catalytic activity. Our present study established the dominant role of CQDs decorated photo-electrodes in solar energy conversion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rineng.2023.101611.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.-B. Kim, J.-Y. Park, C.-S. Kim, K. Okuyama, S.-E. Lee, H.-D. Jang, T.-O. Kim, Effects of Graphene in Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells Based on Nitrogen-Doped TiO 2 Composite, J. Phys. Chem. C. 119 (2015) 16552-16559. doi:10.1021/acs.jpcc.5b02309.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Woo Kim, R. Cheruku, S. Thogiti, B. Min, J. Hong Kim, Nanocrystalline MoO 3 /Polymerized Dibromo-EDOT as Hybrid Nanocomposite: Efficient Hole Transport Material for Solid-State Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells, ChemNanoMat. 5 (2019) 738-747. doi:10.1002/cnma.201900023.
